reasoning: The threshold for a positive resolution requires the number of fatalities in a 30-day window to exceed 10 times the baseline (which is currently ~586/month). This means Burkina Faso would need to experience nearly 5,860+ fatalities in a single month. For context, the deadliest recent event in the country (the Barsalogho massacre in August 2024) killed around 600 people, and total annual civilian fatalities linked to militant Islamists in recent years have ranged between 1,000 and 2,000. Normal conflict deaths put the monthly average at ~585. Scaling the violence up by an order of magnitude to almost 6,000 deaths in one month is unprecedented for this conflict and would be equivalent to almost the entire annual death toll occurring in just 30 days. There have been no localized events reported in March 2025 that approximate this level of violence, precluding positive resolution for the near-term dates (April 2025). While distant dates always carry slightly higher macro uncertainty, achieving 10x an already-high fatality baseline over a single month remains highly implausible. Thus, probabilities are extremely low and set near the bottom 5% bound, scaling up only minutely for the longer-term horizons to respect maximum-uncertainty guidelines.
